course_info.title: Psychology of Technology and Digital Behavior

class_schedule.topic: Cyberpsychology: The Intersection of Technology and Human Behavior
learning_objectives: Define cyberpsychology and its scope in the digital age
Describe how affordances of digital environments shape behavior
Evaluate the online disinhibition effect and its dual nature
readings: Suler, 'The Online Disinhibition Effect' (CyberPsychology & Behavior, 2004); Amichai-Hamburger, Internet Psychology, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital technology has fundamentally transformed human behavior. Cyberpsychology examines how digital environments create new contexts for identity, relationships, cognition, and psychopathology.
key_concepts: Affordances: features of digital environments that enable and constrain behavior
Online disinhibition effect: benign (self-disclosure, empathy) and toxic (hostility, trolling)
Anonymity, asynchronicity, invisibility as psychological mechanisms
Digital dualism vs. augmented reality framework: online life IS real life
Research challenges: ecological validity, privacy, rapidly evolving platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare self-disclosure patterns across anonymous vs. identified online platforms using published data

review.value: Suler: anonymity, invisibility, asynchronicity, and lack of authority online reduce psychological barriers to behavior, producing both prosocial (benign) and antisocial (toxic) disinhibition
Features of a technology that invite certain behaviors: the 'like' button affords social validation; anonymity affords honest self-expression or trolling

learning_objectives: Evaluate the evidence linking social media use to mental health outcomes
Distinguish between active and passive social media use
Describe the social comparison and fear of missing out (FOMO) mechanisms
readings: Twenge, 'Have Smartphones Destroyed a Generation?' (rebuttal: Orben & Przybylski, 2019)

lecture_notes.introduction: Does social media make us depressed? The answer is more nuanced than headlines suggest. Understanding the specific mechanisms and moderators is essential for evidence-based conclusions.
key_concepts: Passive vs. active use: scrolling and consuming vs. posting and interacting
Social comparison theory online: upward comparison with curated self-presentations
Fear of missing out (FOMO): anxiety from perceiving others' rewarding experiences
Effect sizes: Orben & Przybylski -- social media explains less than 0.4% of variance in well-being
Moderators: age, pre-existing mental health, type of platform, quality of use
lecture_notes.demonstration: Critically evaluate two opposing research articles on social media and depression: identify methodological strengths and weaknesses of each

review.value: Active use (posting, messaging, creating) is associated with neutral to positive well-being; passive use (scrolling, lurking) is associated with social comparison and lower well-being
Orben & Przybylski: technology use explains 0.4% of variance in well-being, comparable to eating potatoes; the association exists but is small

class_schedule.topic: Digital Cognition: Attention, Multitasking, and the Google Effect
learning_objectives: Evaluate evidence on how digital technology affects attention and multitasking
Describe the Google effect (cognitive offloading to search engines)
Discuss the impacts of smartphone use on academic performance
readings: Sparrow et al., 'Google Effects on Memory' (Science, 2011); Rosen, 'The Distracted Mind'

lecture_notes.introduction: How does constant connectivity affect how we think? Digital technology may be changing patterns of attention, memory, and learning in ways we are only beginning to understand.
key_concepts: Media multitasking: chronic heavy multitaskers show deficits in filtering, working memory, and task switching
Cognitive offloading: relying on technology as external memory (Google, GPS, contacts)
Sparrow's transactive memory: we remember where to find information, not the information itself
Mere presence effect: smartphone presence reduces available cognitive capacity (Ward et al., 2017)
Reading comprehension: print vs. screen (shallowing hypothesis)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Experimental demonstration: test recall accuracy with and without smartphone present on desk

review.value: Sparrow et al.: when people expect information to be available online, they show better memory for WHERE to find it but worse memory for the information itself
Ward et al.: mere presence of a smartphone on the desk reduces available cognitive capacity, even when the phone is face-down and silenced

learning_objectives: Describe how digital platforms shape identity construction and self-presentation
Compare online and offline relationship formation and maintenance
Discuss catfishing, deceptive self-presentation, and authenticity online
readings: Walther, 'Computer-Mediated Communication' (hyperpersonal model); Turkle, Alone Together, Ch. 8-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital environments create new spaces for identity experimentation and relationship formation, with both liberating and concerning implications.
key_concepts: Goffman's impression management extended to digital contexts
Walther's hyperpersonal model: CMC can produce idealized impressions and intensified relationships
Social media as identity performance: curated self-presentation
Online relationship formation: hyperpersonal communication, idealization, and disillusionment
Authenticity paradox: pressure to present 'real self' while knowing audience is watching
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze self-presentation strategies across three platforms (LinkedIn, Instagram, dating app) for the same hypothetical person

review.value: Walther: CMC's selective self-presentation, idealized perception, and feedback loops can produce relationships more intimate than equivalent face-to-face interactions
Marwick & Boyd: social media merges audiences (friends, family, coworkers, strangers) that are normally separate, creating tension in self-presentation

class_schedule.topic: Problematic Internet Use, Gaming Disorder, and Cyberbullying
learning_objectives: Evaluate the evidence for internet addiction and gaming disorder as diagnostic categories
Describe the psychology of cyberbullying: prevalence, mechanisms, and intervention
Discuss problematic smartphone use and nomophobia
readings: WHO ICD-11 Gaming Disorder criteria; Kowalski et al., Cyberbullying, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: When does healthy technology use become problematic? This is one of the most debated questions in modern psychology, with implications for clinical practice, public policy, and parenting.
key_concepts: Gaming disorder (ICD-11): impaired control, increasing priority, continuation despite consequences
Problematic internet use: conceptual debates -- is it a unique disorder or a symptom?
Cyberbullying: repetition, power imbalance, and intentionality in digital contexts
Bystander effect online: disinhibition and diffusion of responsibility in digital spaces
Nomophobia: fear of being without mobile phone; physiological stress responses documented
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate case vignettes: distinguish problematic gaming from high engagement using ICD-11 criteria

review.value: ICD-11: pattern of persistent gaming behavior (online or offline) characterized by impaired control, increasing priority over other activities, and continuation despite negative consequences, for at least 12 months
Anonymity reduces empathy, asynchronicity allows deliberate cruelty, permanence and publicness amplify harm, and power imbalances are less visible online

class_schedule.topic: AI, Persuasive Design, and the Ethics of Digital Psychology
learning_objectives: Describe persuasive design techniques used in apps and platforms
Explain how AI and algorithmic curation affect behavior and beliefs
Discuss ethical responsibilities of psychologists in technology design and digital well-being
readings: Harris, 'How Technology Hijacks People's Minds'; Zuboff, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology companies employ psychological principles to maximize engagement. Understanding these techniques is essential for both protecting public well-being and informing ethical technology design.
key_concepts: Persuasive design: variable ratio reinforcement (notifications), social validation loops, infinite scroll
Dark patterns: interface designs that manipulate user behavior against their interests
Algorithmic curation: filter bubbles, echo chambers, and radicalization pathways
Surveillance capitalism: behavioral data extraction as business model
Digital well-being: screen time tools, right to disconnect, humane technology movement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a popular app for persuasive design techniques and dark patterns; propose humane redesigns

review.value: Pull-to-refresh, notification checking, and social media feeds use variable ratio schedules -- the most engagement-producing reinforcement pattern, analogous to slot machines
Design choices that trick users: confirm-shaming, hidden unsubscribe buttons, default opt-in to data sharing, roach motels (easy to enter, hard to leave)

questions.question: Explain the 'Google effect' on memory (Sparrow et al., 2011). Is this phenomenon harmful, adaptive, or both? Justify your position with reference to theories of transactive memory.
questions.answer: The Google effect (digital amnesia) is the tendency to forget information that can be easily found online while better remembering where to find it. Sparrow et al. showed that when people expect information will be available digitally, they invest less effort in encoding the content but more in encoding the source. This can be understood through transactive memory theory (Wegner): couples and teams distribute memory responsibilities, remembering who knows what rather than everything themselves. The internet has become a transactive memory partner. This is adaptive because it frees cognitive resources for higher-order thinking, but potentially harmful because it may reduce deep encoding of foundational knowledge needed for creative problem-solving and critical thinking. The net effect likely depends on what information is offloaded.

rubric.key: Accurately describes the Google effect and Sparrow's findings
Provides balanced evaluation with nuanced reasoning

questions.question: A school district wants to ban smartphones to improve academic performance. Using research evidence, evaluate this proposal. Under what conditions might a ban be effective, and under what conditions might it be counterproductive?
questions.answer: Evidence supports that smartphone presence impairs academic performance. Ward et al. (2017) showed that merely having a phone visible reduces cognitive capacity. Beland and Murphy (2016) found that banning phones in schools improved test scores, especially for lower-achieving students. A ban may be effective when: (1) it reduces distraction during instruction, (2) it eliminates social media comparison during the school day, (3) it is consistently enforced. A ban may be counterproductive when: (1) phones are used as legitimate educational tools (research, polling, collaboration), (2) students with anxiety disorders use phones as safety signals and sudden removal increases distress, (3) enforcement creates adversarial student-teacher dynamics. A nuanced policy (phones collected during class, available at breaks) may be more effective than a total ban.

rubric.key: Identifies conditions where ban may be counterproductive

quizzes.topic: Online Identity, Problematic Use, and Digital Ethics

questions.question: Describe three specific persuasive design techniques used by social media platforms and explain the psychological principle each exploits.
questions.answer: (1) Pull-to-refresh and notification badges exploit variable ratio reinforcement: unpredictable rewards (like a slot machine) produce the highest engagement because each check might yield something rewarding. (2) Like/heart counts exploit social validation and social comparison: seeing quantified approval activates reward circuits and creates a feedback loop where users post more to receive more validation. (3) Infinite scroll exploits the Zeigarnik effect and loss aversion: removing natural stopping points (page breaks) prevents the completion signal that would prompt disengagement. Users keep scrolling because the stream feels unfinished. Additional: streaks (Snapchat) exploit loss aversion and the sunk cost fallacy; autoplay exploits status quo bias and inertia.

rubric.key: Identifies three distinct persuasive design techniques
Correctly names the psychological principle for each

questions.question: A parent asks you whether their teenager's 4-hour daily gaming habit constitutes gaming disorder. What questions would you ask to make a determination, based on the ICD-11 criteria?
questions.answer: Time spent alone is insufficient for diagnosis. Based on ICD-11 criteria, I would ask: (1) Impaired control: Can the teen stop gaming when asked or when other obligations arise, or do they continue despite intending to stop? (2) Increasing priority: Has gaming progressively taken precedence over previously valued activities (sports, socializing, homework)? (3) Continuation despite consequences: Has the teen continued gaming despite clear negative outcomes (failing grades, lost friendships, family conflict)? (4) Duration: Has this pattern persisted for at least 12 months? (5) Functional impairment: Is the gaming pattern causing significant impairment in personal, family, social, educational, or occupational functioning? If the teen games 4 hours daily but maintains grades, friendships, and other activities, this is high engagement, not disorder. Diagnosis requires functional impairment, not just high usage.

rubric.key: Lists questions aligned with ICD-11 criteria
Notes that time alone is insufficient for diagnosis

major_assignments.title: Digital Technology Impact Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a specific digital technology or platform and conduct a comprehensive psychological analysis of its impact on users. Include persuasive design analysis, empirical evidence review, effects on specific populations, and evidence-based recommendations for healthier use or design improvements.
objectives: Analyze a technology's psychological mechanisms and effects using published research
Evaluate the quality and limitations of existing evidence
Consider differential effects across populations (age, mental health status)
Propose evidence-based recommendations grounded in psychological science
deliverables: Persuasive design audit of the chosen technology
Recommendations for users, parents, clinicians, and/or designers

criteria: Identifies persuasive design techniques with correct principles
Thorough psychological analysis of technology's mechanisms and effects
Practical evidence-based recommendations for multiple stakeholders

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of digital psychology concepts and research

technology_audits.description: Hands-on analysis of digital platforms using psychological frameworks

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering social media, digital cognition, and online identity

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including problematic use, AI, and digital ethics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Technology audit activities require in-class participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Technology impact papers will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Technology audits may be conducted in pairs. Written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Screenshots used in audits must not violate platform terms of service or user privacy.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to digital platforms for analysis, university library databases, and presentation software.
